source_paper_title: Riemannian Anderson Mixing Methods for Minimizing C2 Functions on Riemannian Manifolds
context:
Page 10, Section 4 ("Regularied RAM and Global Convergence Analysis"); also reiterated on page 14, Section 5.1.

The global convergence of Anderson mixing remains an problem, even in the Euclidean setting.
title: Global convergence guarantees for Anderson mixing in Euclidean space
latex:
Given an integer memory parameter \(m\ge 1\), a mapping \(g:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\), and a damping sequence \((\beta_k)_{k\ge 0}\) with \(\beta_k>0\), consider the Anderson mixing iteration of depth \(m\) defined as follows.

Let \(x_0\in\mathbb{R}^n\) be arbitrary and for each \(k\ge 0\) define the residual \(r_k:=g(x_k)-x_k\). Let \(m_k:=\min\{m,k\}\). For \(j=1,\dots,m_k\), define the differences \(\Delta x_{k-j}:=x_{k-j+1}-x_{k-j}\) and \(\Delta r_{k-j}:=r_{k-j+1}-r_{k-j}\), and form matrices
\[
X_k := [\Delta x_{k-m_k},\ldots,\Delta x_{k-1}]\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m_k},\qquad
R_k := [\Delta r_{k-m_k},\ldots,\Delta r_{k-1}]\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m_k}.
\]
Compute coefficients \(\Gamma_k\in\mathbb{R}^{m_k}\) as a least-squares solution
\[
\Gamma_k\in\arg\min_{\Gamma\in\mathbb{R}^{m_k}} \|r_k - R_k\Gamma\|_2,
\]
set \(\bar r_k:=r_k-R_k\Gamma_k\), and update
\[
 x_{k+1} := x_k + \beta_k r_k - (X_k+\beta_k R_k)\Gamma_k.
\]

Problem: give verifiable assumptions on \(g\), \(m\), and \((\beta_k)\) under which the above Anderson mixing sequence \((x_k)\) is globally convergent to a fixed point \(x^\star\in\mathbb{R}^n\) satisfying \(g(x^\star)=x^\star\), for every initial point \(x_0\in\mathbb{R}^n\). In particular, characterize conditions ensuring (i) existence of a fixed point \(x^\star\), (ii) well-posedness of the least-squares problems for all \(k\), and (iii) \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \|x_k-x^\star\|_2=0\) (or \(\lim_{k\to\infty}\|r_k\|_2=0\)).
location_in_paper: Page 10, Section 4 ("Regularied RAM and Global Convergence Analysis"); also reiterated on page 14, Section 5.1.
open_problem_quote: The global convergence of Anderson mixing remains an problem, even in the Euclidean setting.
